title: War by Other Means
gist: As robots and AI substitute for soldiers, the source of state coercive power shifts from mass labor to the private firms (Palantir, Anthropic, SpaceX, Google) that build and operate war machines, eroding the popular constraints that historically disciplined the use of force and forcing a rewrite of the liberal-democratic social contract.

themes.theme: Robotic warfare as proven battlefield reality

insights.claim: In December 2024 Ukraine's 13th Khartiia Brigade overran Russian positions near Lyptsi, cleared mines, and held captured ground entirely with unmanned ground vehicles and drones, with no Ukrainian soldier physically present on the battlefield.
insights.speaker: John Severini
insights.so_what: A fully robotic combined-arms assault has already happened; treat unmanned combined-arms operations as a deployed capability, not a future scenario, when sizing defense-tech demand.
insights.quote: But not a single Ukrainian was present on the battlefield.
insights.locator: In December 2024, the Ukrainian National Guard's 13th Khartiia Brigade

insights.claim: Both Ukraine and Russia have fielded fully autonomous drones confirmed to have killed human combatants, marking the early normalization of machine-run autonomous lethality on the battlefield.
insights.speaker: John Severini
insights.so_what: Autonomous lethal weapons have crossed from theory to confirmed combat use; expect accelerating procurement and regulatory friction around autonomy.
insights.quote: both sides of the war in Ukraine have fielded fully autonomous drones, which are confirmed to have taken the lives of human combatants
insights.locator: A gap between human and machine capabilities remains

insights.claim: Electronic jamming has become so widespread that both Ukraine and Russia rely on lightweight fiber-optic tethers to maintain control links, a workaround that limits drone range and mobility.
insights.speaker: John Severini
insights.so_what: Jam-resistant comms (fiber-optic, autonomy) are the binding constraint on drone effectiveness; watch suppliers solving the link problem.
insights.quote: Both Ukraine and Russia now use lightweight fiber-optic tethers to maintain physical connections, limiting range and mobility.
insights.locator: A gap between human and machine capabilities remains

insights.claim: Machines hold a decisive edge over human soldiers in battlefield data handling because robot-to-robot transmission is immediate and precise and automated command centers process more information than any human operator.
insights.speaker: John Severini
insights.so_what: The military advantage is migrating to data/command-and-control software, not just hardware platforms; the value pools sit with the C2 and machine-vision layer.
insights.quote: data transmission between robots is immediate and precise
insights.locator: Although humans remain predominant on the battlefield

insights.claim: The military 'tooth' is expected to become increasingly machine-run while humans recede into a 'tail' of maintenance, refinement, and training, inverting the traditional manpower structure of armed forces.
insights.speaker: John Severini
insights.so_what: Defense labor demand shifts from frontline headcount to high-skill technical sustainment; model defense spend toward capital and specialized labor, away from personnel.
insights.quote: the military “tooth” may increasingly become machine-run, with humans receding into a “tail” involving maintenance, refinement, and training
insights.locator: A gap between human and machine capabilities remains

insights.claim: A state's pace of fielding robotic soldiers is set by the marginal value it places on military effectiveness versus its scarce capital and labor reserves, making robotics rational where manpower is scarce and irrational where it is abundant.
insights.speaker: John Severini
insights.so_what: Demographics and microelectronics access predict which militaries will pull hardest on defense-tech; map adoption demand to labor-scarce, capital-rich states.
insights.quote: the tradeoff between the two depends on the marginal value a state places on military effectiveness relative to the value it places on its capital and labor reserves
insights.locator: While human soldiers remain more versatile

insights.claim: Ukraine's acute demographic crisis from outmigration, aging, and war casualties makes even inferior robotic soldiers a worthwhile investment because they preserve a limited supply of workers and conscripts.
insights.speaker: John Severini
insights.so_what: Ukraine is structurally forced into defense-tech adoption regardless of quality; it remains a durable demand center and live testbed for unmanned systems.
insights.quote: even inferior robotic soldiers will be a worthwhile investment if it means preserving a limited supply of workers and conscripts
insights.locator: While human soldiers remain more versatile

insights.claim: Labor-rich Russia may rationally forgo advanced robotic soldiers because its willingness to mass-mobilize reserves creates a functional labor surplus relative to its capital-constrained access to advanced microelectronics and high-tech components.
insights.speaker: John Severini
insights.so_what: Russia's chip constraints, not just doctrine, cap its robotic adoption; export controls on advanced microelectronics retain strategic bite.
insights.quote: its willingness to mass-mobilize military reserves creates a functional labor surplus relative to its capital-constrained access to advanced microelectronics and high-tech components
insights.locator: Conversely, a comparatively labor-rich state like Russia

insights.claim: An 'America First' foreign-policy vision holds that downsizing US personnel abroad can be offset by greater use of unmanned munitions, lowering costs and bringing soldiers home without weakening deterrence.
insights.speaker: John Severini
insights.so_what: US policy momentum favors substituting unmanned systems for forward personnel; a tailwind for unmanned-munitions manufacturers tied to deterrence posture.
insights.quote: downsizing American personnel abroad could be amply made up for by increasing the use of unmanned munitions, driving costs down and bringing soldiers home without weakening deterrence
insights.locator: The growing use of drones is both a cause and a consequence

themes.theme: Private firms supplant the state as the source of coercive power

insights.claim: Palantir has been integral to Ukraine's war effort since CEO Alex Karp visited Zelensky in 2022, and during the 2023 counteroffensive its software helped Ukrainian forces track Russian positions in real time and target units with machine-vision algorithms.
insights.speaker: John Severini
insights.so_what: Palantir is a proven, battle-validated provider of targeting and ISR software in active conflict; combat track record underwrites its defense-software franchise.
insights.quote: Palantir’s software helped Ukrainian forces track Russian positions in real time and target units using machine-vision algorithms
insights.locator: Outsourcing War

insights.claim: Private defense-tech firms compete directly with government institutions in providing core military functions and public goods, drawing military power away from public institutions and toward private enterprise.
insights.speaker: John Severini
insights.so_what: The defense value chain is privatizing at the core, not the periphery; firms supplying core military functions capture structurally rising rents and leverage.
insights.quote: firms like Palantir compete with government institutions in the provision of public goods and core military functions
insights.locator: These dynamics point toward a transformed political landscape

insights.claim: The intense efforts by Ukraine and Russia to maintain Starlink access show that the personal foreign-policy choices of CEOs like Elon Musk can decisively shape modern military conflicts.
insights.speaker: John Severini
insights.so_what: Critical military connectivity sits with a single private actor; concentration risk in commercial space comms is now a national-security variable.
insights.quote: how much sway the foreign policy of CEOs like Elon Musk can hold over modern military conflicts
insights.locator: This reliance comes with built-in leverage

insights.claim: Post-Cold-War pressure to cut US defense spending as a share of GDP was achieved largely by outsourcing core functions like weapon-system design, aircraft maintenance, and intelligence to the private sector, where the required expertise increasingly resided.
insights.speaker: John Severini
insights.so_what: US defense outsourcing is a 30-year structural trend, not a recent anomaly; the privatized base is entrenched and hard to reverse.
insights.quote: many core roles—the design of weapon systems, aircraft maintenance, intelligence functions—remained outsourced
insights.locator: Great powers like the United States are just as beholden

insights.claim: Private firms, free of public pay scales and hiring bureaucracy, can outcompete governments for the highly skilled labor that complex military technology demands, especially in AI where the military depends on commercial firms for the best models, data infrastructure, and talent.
insights.speaker: John Severini
insights.so_what: The talent moat keeps frontier AI and weapons capability in private hands; commercial labs hold durable bargaining power over their government customers.
insights.quote: Private firms, unconstrained by public pay scales and hiring bureaucracy, can outcompete governments for the highly skilled labor that complex military technologies demand
insights.locator: Private firms, unconstrained by public pay scales

insights.claim: Dual-use technology deepens the military's dependence on the private sector by giving firms flexible access to both military and civilian markets, reducing their need for government contracts.
insights.speaker: John Severini
insights.so_what: Dual-use firms can walk away from defense work, raising government's effective price and weakening its leverage; favor suppliers with civilian revenue optionality.
insights.quote: the increasing importance of dual-use technology, which gives the private sector flexible access to both military and non-military markets
insights.locator: This imbalance is reinforced

themes.theme: Pricing power, lock-in, and the limits of state control

insights.claim: SpaceX and the Pentagon haggled over the 'subscription tier' for controlling unmanned munitions during the recent Iran war, and the dispute roughly doubled the cost per drone mid-conflict.
insights.speaker: John Severini
insights.so_what: Subscription/control-layer pricing can erase the cost savings of unmanned systems mid-war; recurring-revenue lock-in is the real margin for defense-tech vendors.
insights.quote: each drone becoming twice as expensive in the middle of hostilities
insights.locator: This has led to scenes such as SpaceX and the Pentagon haggling

insights.claim: Project Maven grew from a $70 million contract (with $9 million for Google) into a $1.5 billion program, of which nearly $800 million was fulfilled by Palantir after Google exited.
insights.speaker: John Severini
insights.so_what: Military AI-planning programs scale roughly 20x once embedded; Palantir captured the dominant share of Maven, a template for defense-software TAM expansion.
insights.quote: Maven began as a $70 million contract with $9 million earmarked for Google, but it grew into a program worth $1.5 billion, with nearly $800 million of it fulfilled by Palantir.
insights.locator: Fortunes are often at stake

insights.claim: Mission-critical military software like Project Maven becomes hard to customize or replace without ongoing access to its developers, driving Palantir's 'forward-deployed engineer' model and creating deep vendor lock-in.
insights.speaker: John Severini
insights.so_what: Forward-deployed engineering converts software contracts into sticky, hard-to-displace relationships; lock-in is the structural source of defense-software pricing power.
insights.quote: the “forward-deployed engineer” model popularized by Palantir
insights.locator: Take software development, for instance

insights.claim: Vendors are seeking to retain ownership rights over the algorithms and data embedded in military systems, meaning core strategic and operational planning is partly determined by actors who do not represent the state.
insights.speaker: John Severini
insights.so_what: IP ownership of military planning systems is becoming a contested asset; whoever holds the algorithms holds enduring leverage over the customer.
insights.quote: the political calculus inherent to such planning is partly determined by actors who do not represent the state
insights.locator: Considering how deeply software is integrated

insights.claim: As military software grows more complex, the tacit knowledge needed even to audit these systems becomes entrenched within the firm, escalating the transaction costs for the state to reassert control over time.
insights.speaker: John Severini
insights.so_what: State-vendor power asymmetry compounds; the longer development is delegated, the more permanent the dependence and the vendor's pricing power.
insights.quote: the tacit knowledge necessary to even audit these systems becomes further entrenched within the firm
insights.locator: If the state continues to delegate development

insights.claim: Nationalization is an illusory remedy because a defense firm's strategic value lies in its organizational capacity for rapid innovation, which collapses once seized, raising the question of how many Anthropic staff would stay if the US government nationalized it.
insights.speaker: John Severini
insights.so_what: The state's ultimate fallback (nationalization) is hollow against talent-driven firms; private defense-tech leverage is more durable than commonly assumed.
insights.quote: Nationalizing these industries risks destroying the market-driven incentive structures that generate their comparative advantage in the first place
insights.locator: One might argue that the state ultimately retains the trump card

themes.theme: Fragility and elite leverage of public-private war partnerships

insights.claim: Tech firms can unilaterally walk away from defense contracts over employee backlash or shifting priorities, as Google did when it exited Project Maven in 2018 after employee protests.
insights.speaker: John Severini
insights.so_what: Internal employee politics is a real contract-termination risk for defense-tech; vendor reliability varies with workforce culture, a diligence factor for procurement.
insights.quote: Google’s decision to exit Project Maven in 2018 after employee protests was an early demonstration of how fragile these partnerships can be.
insights.locator: Managing this relationship remains a constant balancing act

insights.claim: The dispute between Anthropic and the Department of War over AI's use in autonomous weaponry is escalating from sanction into legal dispute, even as the US military continued to use Anthropic's Claude during its strikes on Iran.
insights.speaker: John Severini
insights.so_what: Even AI providers in active legal conflict with the government remain operationally indispensable; usage persists through disputes, underscoring lock-in.
insights.quote: That the U.S. military continued to use Anthropic’s Claude during its strikes on Iran shouldn’t come as a surprise.
insights.locator: As the disagreement between Anthropic and the Department of War

insights.claim: War-machine creators can already signal disagreement with state policy—as with Anthropic over Iran or Musk denying Ukraine Starlink access in Crimean waters—but their leverage is currently limited by a lack of inter-elite coordination.
insights.speaker: John Severini
insights.so_what: The check on tech-elite power today is fragmentation, not law; coordinated action among defense-tech principals would be a step-change in private geopolitical power to watch.
insights.quote: Musk denying Ukraine access to Starlink in Crimean waters, but for now there is a lack of inter-elite coordination on such matters
insights.locator: Today, the creators of war machines can take actions

insights.claim: Historically the social contract rested on the sovereign needing to wield its citizens' coercive potential, so abuses provoked draft-dodging, desertion, and mutiny—constraints that disappear once robots, not people, supply coercive force.
insights.speaker: John Severini
insights.so_what: Robotic force removes the historic popular veto on state violence, structurally insulating governments from revolt; a long-horizon political-risk regime change.
insights.quote: Draft-dodging, desertion, and mutiny are all manifestations of this—each has been seen in the Ukraine war—but they are solved problems when robots predominate.
insights.locator: This bargain, however, entails a recognition

insights.claim: As machines substitute for human labor on the battlefield, the source of coercive capacity and the vector of political upheaval shift away from labor and toward capital, breaking the old social contract once the sovereign no longer needs to co-opt citizens' coercive capacity.
insights.speaker: John Severini
insights.so_what: Political power follows the locus of coercive capacity; expect rising leverage for capital-holders of military tech and weakening of mass-labor political bargaining.
insights.quote: the source of coercive capacity and the vector of political upheaval will correspondingly continue to shift away from labor and toward capital
insights.locator: If this trend toward substituting machines for human labor continues

insights.claim: The nuclear revolution, computerization, and precision targeting reversed the advantage of mass armies, privileging high-tech capital and pushing powerful states into large-scale industrial and R&D investment to stay competitive.
insights.speaker: John Severini
insights.so_what: The decline of mass armies is a decades-old structural trend now culminating in robotics; sustained defense R&D and industrial investment is the competitive baseline.
insights.quote: the shrinking marginal value of labor reversed the advantage of mass armies
insights.locator: With the advent of the nuclear revolution

insights.claim: Countries like Ukraine are especially vulnerable to becoming beholden to foreign capital under the new social contract, and only continued development of domestic high-skilled labor and industry can prevent it.
insights.speaker: John Severini
insights.so_what: Sovereignty now correlates with a domestic defense-tech and high-skill base; nations lacking it trade strategic autonomy for foreign-capital dependence.
insights.quote: it will take the continued development of domestic high-skilled labor and industry to prevent them from being beholden to the interests of foreign capital
insights.locator: Countries like Ukraine are especially vulnerable

insights.claim: Removing humans from the battlefield lowers the political cost of war, making it more palatable and routine and creating the disjunction between military effort and political consequence that produces 'forever wars.'
insights.speaker: John Severini
insights.so_what: Cheaper, casualty-free force lowers the threshold for conflict; expect more frequent, lower-profile military engagements as unmanned systems proliferate.
insights.quote: “Forever wars” emerge from such a disjunction between military effort and political consequence.
insights.locator: Slowly but surely, the absence of humans on the battlefield
